titleStmt.title: A Transcription of John in Middle Fayumic
P. Mich. inv. 3521
The New Testament
The Gospel according to John
john

respStmt.name: the International Greek New Testament Project
Christian Askeland
Brice Jones

sponsor.name: The Institute for Textual Scholarship and Electronic Editing

publisher.name: The Institute for Textual Scholarship and Electronic Editing
University of Birmingham

availability.p: Licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International License (CC BY 4.0): available for re-use provided attribution is made to the original creators (Christian Askeland and any named participants) and an indication is given of any changes made.

msDesc.surrogates: The images to which this transcription is linked are digitally reproduced with the permission of the Papyrology Collection, Graduate Library, University of Michigan (inventory number 3521): see further http://www.lib.umich.edu/papyrus-collection

sourceDesc.additional: Husselman, Elinor Mullett, ed. The gospel of John in Fayumic Coptic (P.Mich. Inv. 3521). Ann Arbor: Kelsey Museum, 1962. Metzger, Bruce Manning. “review of The gospel of John in Fayumic Coptic (P. Mich. Inv. 3521) by Elinor M. Husselman.” Journal of Biblical Literature 82, no. 3 (1963): 345–346. Polotsky, Hans Jacob. “review of Husselman, The gospel of John in Fayumic Coptic (P.Mich. Inv. 3521).” Orientalistische Literaturzeitung 59, no. 5 (1964): 250–253.

projectDesc.p: This transcription was made by Christian Askeland and Brice Jones for the International Greek New Testament Project in its work towards the Editio Critica Maior of John in 2011.

editorialDecl.p: The initial transcription file was a plain text file later converted to XML. Further information about the transcription procedures followed may be found in the electronic edition of the majuscules at
The XML specifications used were version 1.5 of the IGNTP Guidelines available at

revisionDesc.change: Converted to XML specifications 1.5 by Hugh Houghton
Image links corrected by Hugh Houghton
Converted to XML by Catherine Smith and Hugh Houghton
